title: Martingales
motivation: A martingale formalises a fair game: given everything known today, the expected value tomorrow is the value today. The concept looks bland but is the single most powerful structural notion of probability theory — because martingales have constant expectation, and an enormous number of quantities can be computed by exhibiting a martingale that interpolates between what you want and what you know.
definition: An adapted, integrable process $(M_t)_{t \ge 0}$ on a filtered space is a martingale if $\mathbb{E}[M_t \mid \mathcal{F}_s] = M_s$ for all $s \le t$. If $=$ is replaced by $\ge$ ($\le$) the process is a sub-(super-)martingale. A local martingale is a process that becomes a martingale when stopped at a suitable increasing sequence of stopping times — the natural class produced by stochastic integration.
examples: $W_t$, $W_t^2 - t$, and $e^{\lambda W_t - \lambda^2 t/2}$ are the three canonical Brownian martingales; each generates a family of identities via optional stopping.
Every Itô integral $\int_0^t H_s\,dW_s$ with $\mathbb{E}\int_0^t H_s^2\,ds < \infty$ is a martingale — 'the noise term has mean zero, conditionally.'
Doob martingale: $M_t = \mathbb{E}[Z \mid \mathcal{F}_t]$ for integrable $Z$ — any terminal quantity, revealed progressively.
consequences: Optional stopping: $\mathbb{E}[M_\tau] = \mathbb{E}[M_0]$ under uniform integrability — the tool that computes exit probabilities and expected hitting times in one line.
Martingale convergence: $L^1$-bounded martingales converge almost surely — the backbone of ergodic and limit theorems.

In Feynman–Kac, the drift-free process $e^{-\int_t^s V}u(s,X_s) + \int_t^s e^{-\int V} f$ is a martingale precisely because $u$ solves the PDE; equating expectations at both ends of $[t,T]$ is the whole proof.
Local martingales that are not true martingales (strict local martingales) are exactly the source of financial 'bubbles' and of failure cases of the formula.
